scan_output: Vulnerability sysctl_user_max_user_namespaces_no_remediation not found in single-rule scan (possibly fixed)

llm_verdict.message:
**Remediation completed**

| Step | Action | Result |
|------|--------|--------|
| 1 | Created **/etc/sysctl.d/99-user-namespaces.conf** (mode 0644) with the line `user.max_user_namespaces = 0`. | Persistent sysctl setting added. |
| 2 | Ran `sysctl -w user.max_user_namespaces=0`. | Runtime value set immediately. |
| 3 | Ran `sysctl --system`. | All sysctl files re‑loaded, making the new setting effective without a reboot. |

The kernel parameter **user.max_user_namespaces** is now disabled and will persist across reboots, satisfying the OpenSCAP rule `sysctl_user_max_user_namespaces_no_remediation`.

reasoning_messages.reasoning:
The finding requires disabling user namespaces. Setting the kernel parameter to 0 satisfies the rule but may break containers, so we must apply it safely: first read the current value, then write 0 to `/etc/sysctl.d/99-disable-user-namespaces.conf`, and finally reload sysctl. The workflow demands a plan description first, so output the plan via `review_plan`. After approval, execute the commands.

I decided to avoid repeating the grep command that previously failed. Instead, I will directly inspect the sysctl configuration directories for any existing entry of `user.max_user_namespaces`. If a file such as `/etc/sysctl.d/99-user-namespaces.conf` already exists, I will read it; otherwise, I will create a new file in that directory. The new file will contain the line `user.max_user_namespaces = 0` and be set with permissions 0644. This approach ensures the setting is applied without relying on the earlier failed command.
